skill.name: infsh-cli
skill.description: Run 250+ AI apps via inference.sh CLI - image generation, video creation, LLMs, search, 3D, Twitter automation. Use belt CLI to access FLUX, Veo, Gemini, Grok, Claude, Seedance, OmniHuman, Tavily, Exa, and more.
skill.summary: Access 250+ AI models through a simple CLI - no GPU required.
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: This is legitimate documentation for the inference.sh belt CLI tool. Static findings are false positives: pipe-to-shell commands are standard CLI installation, backtick references are documentation examples, and environment access is standard API authentication. No malicious intent detected. No prompt injection attempts found.

medium_findings.title: CLI Command Execution References (Documentation)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged CLI commands (belt app list, belt app run) as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are legitimate documentation examples showing how to use the CLI tool, not actual code executions. The skill provides reference documentation for a valid AI marketplace CLI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged code patterns are bash command examples in code blocks, not actual shell executions. This is standard documentation format.

low_findings.title: Standard CLI Installation Patterns
low_findings.description: curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh is standard practice for CLI tool installation. The skill documents an official CLI from inference.sh. Alternative manual installation instructions are also provided.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pipe-to-shell is a standard installation method. The URL is from a legitimate service (inference.sh) and the skill provides checksum verification.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to Documentation
low_findings.description: URLs to inference.sh domain are legitimate references to the official service documentation and API endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to inference.sh official documentation and cloud services.

low_findings.title: API Key Environment Variable Documentation
low_findings.description: INFSH_API_KEY environment variable is standard CLI authentication. This is documented best practice, not credential exposure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documenting how to set API keys via environment variables is standard practice for CLI tools.

low_findings.title: Local File Path Examples
low_findings.description: File paths like ~/.local/bin, ./image.png are legitimate examples for local file upload to AI apps.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are standard file path examples for a CLI that supports local file uploads to AI services.

content.value_statement: Running AI models locally requires expensive GPUs. This skill provides CLI access to 250+ AI apps including image generation, video creation, and LLMs through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Generate images with FLUX, Gemini 3, Grok Imagine, and Seedream
Create videos with Veo 3.1, Seedance, Wan 2.5, and OmniHuman
Call LLMs including Claude, Gemini, Kimi, and any OpenRouter model
Perform web search with Tavily and Exa
Automate Twitter/X posts with post-tweet, dm-send, and other endpoints
Upload local files for AI processing - images, audio, and video
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and API credits
Network connection required for all AI operations
Results depend on third-party AI model availability and rate limits
Local file uploads are limited by file size restrictions

use_cases.title: Quick AI Image Generation
use_cases.description: Generate high-quality images without GPU setup. Run FLUX, Gemini, or Grok models with simple CLI commands.
use_cases.target_user: Developers and designers needing quick image generation

use_cases.title: AI-Powered Content Automation
use_cases.description: Automate Twitter posts, generate AI avatars, or create videos from images using CLI workflows.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and social media managers

use_cases.title: Research and Data Collection
use_cases.description: Use Tavily and Exa search to gather web data, extract content, and answer questions using LLM APIs.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Image Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use belt to generate an image of a sunset over mountains using FLUX

prompt_templates.title: Run Claude for Text Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use belt to call Claude Sonnet 4.5 via OpenRouter to explain quantum computing

prompt_templates.title: Create Video from Local Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a sample input for Veo 3.1, then run it with a local image file to create a video

prompt_templates.title: Automated Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Tavily for recent AI news, then use Claude to summarize and post to Twitter automatically

output_examples.input: belt app run falai/flux-dev-lora --input '{"prompt": "a cat astronaut"}'
output_examples.output: Returns image URLs and download links for generated images
Shows task status: running, completed, or failed, with result URLs for file outputs

best_practices: Use belt app sample to generate input files before running apps for the first time
Store your INFSH_API_KEY in environment variables rather than hardcoding
Use --no-wait flag for long-running tasks like video generation to avoid timeouts
anti_patterns: Do not share your INFSH_API_KEY in code or logs
Avoid running tasks in loops without checking task status first
Do not assume AI model outputs are safe - validate outputs before using them

faq.question: What is inference.sh?
faq.answer: inference.sh is a cloud platform that provides API access to 250+ AI models without requiring GPU hardware.

faq.question: How do I authenticate?
faq.answer: Run belt login to authenticate via browser, or set the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable.

faq.question: Can I use local files?
faq.answer: Yes, the CLI automatically uploads local files when you provide a path instead of a URL in your input JSON.

faq.question: What AI models are available?
faq.answer: Models include FLUX, Veo, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Seedance, OmniHuman, Tavily, Exa, and 240+ more.

faq.question: How do I track long-running tasks?
faq.answer: Use --no-wait when running apps, then check status with belt task get <task-id>.

faq.question: Is there a free tier?
faq.answer: Check inference.sh pricing. Some models have free credits for new users.
